headline: The Sabah Claim That Won't Die — What the Philippines Wants, What Malaysia Won, and What Nobody Settled
context: The Philippines reaffirmed its sovereignty claim over Sabah in February 2026. Malaysia has won every court case — Paris, The Hague, Netherlands — but all on procedure. No court has ruled on the 1878 'ceded vs leased' question because Malaysia has never consented to ICJ jurisdiction.

cards.big: The Philippines still claims Sabah. Malaysia has won every court case. The question no court has answered: was the 1878 agreement a sale or a lease?
cards.sub: In February 2026, the Philippines' DFA reaffirmed sovereignty over Sabah after a NAMRIA cartograph depicted it as Philippine territory. Malaysia won a $14.9B award annulment in Paris (Dec 2025), a Dutch Supreme Court ruling (2024), and a Hague dismissal (2023). All were procedural. The ICJ has never ruled on the merits.

cards.big: One word — 'ceded' or 'leased' — in an 1878 document has generated 148 years of dispute. Malaysia's position is overwhelmingly stronger. But no court has been asked to rule.
cards.sub: The English text says 'grant and cede.' The Sulu text uses 'pajakkan' — contested as 'lease.' Every Malaysian court win was about arbitration procedure. Malaysia has never consented to ICJ jurisdiction. The Philippines' RA5446 (1968) preserves the claim in law — no president has repealed it.

cards.big: In 2013, armed followers of a Sulu claimant invaded eastern Sabah. 78 casualties. The claim is not abstract — it has produced armed incursion within living memory.
cards.sub: The Lahad Datu standoff killed 68 militants and 10 Malaysian security personnel. ESSZONE was created. Malaysia stopped annual cession payments it had maintained since 1963. The Philippines distanced itself but never renounced the underlying claim that motivated the incursion.

cards.big: Sabah unanimously rejects the Philippine claim. But 810,000 foreigners — 23.7% of the population — live in the space between two nations' arguments, many undocumented or stateless.
cards.sub: Every Sabah party condemns the claim. But Census 2020 counted 810,443 foreigners in a state of 3.4 million. Philippine CHR and UNICEF document statelessness risk, birth registration barriers, and healthcare exclusion for families of Filipino descent. Bajau Laut homes were demolished in Semporna in 2024. The sovereignty dispute is lived by border communities, not diplomats.

cards.big: The real question is not who owns Sabah — self-determination and 63 years of statehood answer that. The question is why two nations keep a 148-year dispute alive when both know neither will settle it.

Malaysia wins on law. The Philippines loses on law. Neither has closed the dispute. And the people who pay — stateless families, border communities, a state whose priorities get buried every time Manila publishes a map — have no seat at either table.
